questions.question: How should music and movement practices be modified for people with chronic pain?

options.text: Avoid all movement—rest completely
Push through pain to build strength

Use very gentle movement within pain-free range, slow music (60-80 BPM) for nervous system calming, distracting music during pain flares, and mindful movement to rebuild safe relationship with body—respect the pain gate, never force
Only fast, intense music is effective

questions.explanation: Chronic pain modifications: (1) Very gentle movement within pain-free range (respect the gate—never force through pain), (2) slow music (60-80 BPM) activates parasympathetic nervous system and reduces pain perception, (3) distracting music during flares shifts attention away from pain, (4) mindful movement rebuilds trust and safe relationship with body, (5) shorter sessions (5-10 min) prevent fatigue-induced pain flares. Key: movement is medicine for chronic pain, but it must be gentle, paced, and never forced. Moderate evidence for music reducing pain intensity and improving quality of life.

questions.question: What are the most effective music interventions for people with dementia?

options.text: New, complex music to stimulate the brain

Familiar music from their youth (20s-30s), simple repetitive songs, group singing for social connection, and live music when possible—capitalizes on preserved musical memory even when verbal memory is impaired
Loud, fast music to increase energy
No music—dementia patients can't benefit

questions.explanation: STRONG evidence for dementia music interventions: (1) Familiar music from youth (20s-30s) is preserved even when recent memory is lost—triggers memories, emotions, and connection, (2) Simple, repetitive songs allow participation even with cognitive decline, (3) Group singing provides social connection and reduces isolation, (4) Live music (when possible) is more engaging than recorded, (5) Music reduces agitation, anxiety, and depression symptoms. Musical memory is often preserved longer than verbal memory in dementia, making music a powerful tool for connection and quality of life.

questions.question: How should music and movement practices be adapted for autistic individuals?

options.text: Force eye contact and neurotypical social behaviors
Avoid all music due to sensory sensitivities

Honor sensory needs (adjust volume, allow movement, offer ear protection), provide predictability (clear structure, visual schedules), allow choice and control, celebrate strengths (rhythm, pattern recognition), and avoid forced social interaction—neurodivergent-affirming approach
Use only classical music

questions.explanation: Neurodivergent-affirming autism adaptations: (1) Honor sensory needs—adjust volume, allow stimming/movement, offer ear protection for hypersensitivity, (2) Provide predictability—clear structure, visual schedules, explain what will happen, (3) Allow choice and control—no forced participation, offer options, (4) Celebrate strengths—many autistic people have strong rhythm, pattern recognition, or musical ability, (5) No forced social interaction—parallel play/music is valid, (6) Respect communication differences—musical expression may be easier than verbal. Goal is support, not forcing neurotypical norms.

questions.question: What special considerations apply when using music in medical settings (hospitals, rehabilitation)?

options.text: No special considerations—use standard practices

Keep volume very low (hospital noise sensitivity), use patient-preferred music (not imposed), time practices around medical procedures (pre-surgery anxiety, post-procedure pain), coordinate with medical team, ensure infection control, and recognize scope limits (music supports recovery, doesn't replace medical treatment)
Only medical staff can use music
Music has no place in medical settings

questions.explanation: Medical setting considerations: (1) Low volume (hospital environments are already noisy, patients may be sound-sensitive), (2) Patient-preferred music (not imposed—ask preferences), (3) Strategic timing (pre-surgery anxiety reduction, during procedures for distraction, post-procedure pain management), (4) Coordinate with medical team (ensure practices don't interfere with care), (5) Infection control (clean equipment, don't share items), (6) Scope limits—music therapists (MT-BC) deliver clinical interventions; music supports medical treatment but doesn't replace it. MODERATE-STRONG evidence for music reducing pre-surgical anxiety, postoperative pain, and ICU stress.

questions.question: When are music and movement practices contraindicated (should not be used)?

options.text: Never—music is always safe

Acute medical instability (cardiac event, severe pain crisis, active psychosis), severe auditory sensitivity causing distress, when patient explicitly refuses, or when practices would interfere with urgent medical care—always defer to medical team and patient autonomy
For anyone over 65
For people with mental illness

questions.explanation: Contraindications (when NOT to use music/movement): (1) Acute medical instability—cardiac event, severe pain crisis, acute respiratory distress (stabilize first), (2) Active psychosis where music increases agitation or delusions, (3) Severe auditory sensitivity causing distress (some autism/sensory disorders—respect this), (4) Patient explicitly refuses (autonomy always trumps), (5) Interference with urgent medical care (emergency situations). Key: music and movement are generally safe, but medical stability, patient consent, and clinical judgment always come first. When in doubt, defer to medical team and patient wishes.
